titleStmt.title: Letter from William Tyrrell Jr. to Victoria Mary Tyrrell, 18 November 1917
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: William Tyrrell Jr.

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
The letters of William Upton Tyrrell ('Willie') to his father William J.H. Tyrrell, mother Elizabeth, sister Minnie and brother Robert are a notable example of wartime correspondence. The letters - nearly 200 in total - are mostly written by William to his family while in active service with the Royal Irish Rifles in France. Willie discusses his life and the lives of other soldiers in the trenches, as well as life at Ballindoolin, the family estate.
The family name 'Tyrrell' is sometimes misspelled 'Tyrell' in correspondence to the family.
Summary of the present letter:
Willie expresses gratitude for a pheasant.

msIdentifier.repository: OPW-Maynooth University Archive & Research Centre at Castletown

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
18 Nov 1917 My dear Minnie No news but just a line
to tell you all is well. I got a
grand parcel today which was
posted on the 8th. It contained a
pheasant which was in perfect
condition I eat most of it for lunch
What I left I gave to others but
I can assure you they did not get
much. The mortal remains are now
being made into soup for dinner. The
dates & mushroom were
also top hole Particularly the
dates as they are a thing we
never get. Those thick bars of
chocolate are also splendid. I take
them on the march & on such
occasions. I am going to be away from the
battalion again by the time you
get this letter. I am being kept as
a souvenir of the battalion for
after the war. There has been no letter post
for the last two or three days
for some unknown reason.
No trace of Garrett yet.
There is no news at all
so with very best love to
all Your most affec brother
Willie
Service Mrs Tyrrell
Ballindoolin
Edenderry
Kings Co
Ireland W. W. Tyrrell
